ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 represents pear as a whole fruit; brown-skinned variety is a cultivar-level distinction not separately coded in FoodOn.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium brown pear (approximately 166 g) is a typical single serving, providing around 105 kcal and 5.6 g of dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Pear, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for fresh pear (raw). Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 across all nutrient fields. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw pear (FDC ID 169118) was cross-checked and values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 38 (glucose reference = 100) for fresh pear is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 38 × 14.3 g × 1.66 (166 g serving) / 100 ≈ 8.0. Brown pear is a cultivar of Pyrus communis; no cultivar-specific GI deviation is documented, so the species-level GI value is applied.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), moderate potassium (100 mg/100 g), and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Brown pear is appropriate for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and fibre-rich profile support blood pressure management, and it should be consumed as whole fruit rather than juice to preserve fibre and avoid concentrated sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~38), high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g) moderating glucose absorption from 10.4 g natural sugar per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brown pear has a low glycaemic index of approximately 38 and 3.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which slows glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume one medium pear (≈166 g) per serving as part of a balanced diet, in line with ADA fruit guidance, preferring whole fruit over pear juice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat (0 g/100 g), 3.4 g dietary fibre/100 g, and flavonoid polyphenols (quercetin) in skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brown pear contains no saturated or trans fat, provides 3.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g which supports LDL cholesterol reduction, and delivers quercetin and other flavonoid antioxidants concentrated in the skin; consuming the whole fruit with skin intact is recommended for cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (quercetin, flavonoids) in skin and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brown pear provides quercetin and flavonoid polyphenols in the skin alongside 3.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which support gut microbiome diversity relevant to immune regulation in rheumatoid arthritis; eating the pear with skin intact maximises these benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown pear (Pear, brown) is a fresh, raw whole fruit of the species Pyrus communis, characterised by its golden-brown russeted skin, crisp and juicy flesh, and mild sweet flavour with subtle floral notes. It is consumed raw as a snack or dessert fruit and is widely available in Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 63 kcal, 14.3 g carbohydrate (of which 10.4 g is natural fruit sugar), 3.4 g dietary fibre, 0.3 g protein, and negligible fat (0 g). The high fibre content slows glucose absorption, contributing to a low glycaemic index of approximately 38. Sodium is very low at 2 mg/100 g, making it suitable for sodium-restricted diets. The skin contains quercetin and other flavonoid polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Brown pear is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre), hypertension (low sodium, moderate potassium), and cardiovascular disease (zero saturated fat, cholesterol-lowering fibre). It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. A typical serving is one medium pear (approximately 166 g), providing around 105 kcal and 5.6 g dietary fibre. Consuming the whole fruit with skin intact is recommended to maximise fibre and polyphenol intake.
common_user_queries: Is brown pear good for diabetics?
How many calories in a brown pear?
Can I eat pear if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of pear?
Is brown pear the same as Bosc pear?
How much fibre does a pear have?
Is pear suitable for a low-sodium diet?
What are the health benefits of eating pear skin?
How many carbs in a brown pear?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95 across all fields, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169118) for raw pear. GI value of 38 is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). All values are internally consistent.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw pear; this is naturally zero as fresh pear contains negligible fat by composition. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 consistent with the negligible total fat value. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as fresh pear contains no meaningful vitamin D. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient breakdown beyond those reported in the HPB source is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry used; USDA SR Legacy reports water_g ≈ 83.7 g/100 g for raw pear but this was not carried forward as the primary source is HPB. vitamin_c_mg of 4.0 mg/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169118) which reports 4.3 mg/100 g for raw pear; this is consistent with published values and is used as a secondary supporting figure. The high_sugar_warning dietary tag in the input is noted; however, the sugar content (10.4 g/100 g) is from intact whole fruit with 3.4 g dietary fibre, which does not trigger the solid-food added-sugar gate. The tag is retained for informational purposes only. Brown pear is a cultivar of Pyrus communis; no cultivar-specific GI deviation from the species-level GI of 38 is documented in approved sources.
